1: wayyišbĕtû šĕlōšeṯ hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh mēʿănôṯ ʾet-ʾîyôḇ — 'so these three men stopped answering Job' — šāḇat (to cease/stop; the Sabbath-rest verb applied to the friends' exhaustion of arguments). kî hûʾ ṣaddîq bĕʿênāyw — 'because he was righteous in his own eyes' — bĕʿênāyw = 'in his own eyes'; the narrator's framing of the friends' stopping point: they ceased because Job maintained his ṣaddîq status in his own eyes — not because they believed him, but because they could not break his self-assessment. The phrase ṣaddîq bĕʿênāyw will be reprised in Elihu's anger in v2.
2: wayyiḥar ʾap̄ ʾĔlîhû ben-ḇaraḵĕʾēl habbûzî — 'the anger of Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite burned' — ḥārāh ʾap̄ (to be angry; the nostrils-burn idiom — ʾap̄ = both 'nose' and 'anger' because of the visible nostril-flaring of anger; used throughout from Gen 30:2 to Num 11:1). ʾēlîhû = 'He is my God' or 'My God is He'; Barachel = 'God blesses'; Bûzî = from Buz (Nahor's son in Gen 22:21, an Arabian location). wayyiḥar ʾappô bĕʾîyôḇ ʿal-ṣaddĕqô nap̄šô mēʾĔlōhîm — 'angry against Job for justifying himself rather than God' — nepeš (self/soul); ṣādaq in the pi'el = to declare righteous; Elihu is angry that Job justified himself at the expense of God's justice — but also (v3) angry at the friends for failing to answer.
3: wĕḇišĕlōšeṯ rēʿāyw ḥārāh ʾappô — 'he was also angry with his three friends' — the double anger is structurally remarkable: Elihu is enraged both at Job's self-justification AND at the friends' inadequate theodicy. ʿal ʾăšer lōʾ-māṣĕʾû maʿăneh wayyarĕšîʿû ʾeṯ-ʾîyôḇ — 'because they had found no answer and yet condemned Job' — māṣāʾ maʿăneh (to find/discover an answer); rāšaʿ in the hip'il = to declare wicked/condemn; the friends condemned Job without an adequate theological basis for doing so. This is Elihu's charge against the friends: conviction without adequate argument.
4: wĕʾĔlîhû ḥikkāh ʾeṯ-ʾîyôḇ biddĕḇārîm — 'Elihu had waited to speak with/about Job' — ḥikkāh (to wait for eagerly; the same root as tiqwāh's waiting; Ps 33:20 'our soul waits/ḥāḵāh for the LORD'); the technical meaning here is that Elihu waited in queue, holding back his words while the older men finished. kî zĕqēnîm hēmmāh mimmennû lĕyāmîm — 'because they were older than he was in days' — the cultural protocol of age-deference; Elihu has been waiting not from timidity but from respect for seniority, which makes his eventual anger more credible.
5: wayyarʾ ʾĔlîhû kî ʾên maʿăneh bĕp̄î šĕlōšeṯ hāʾănāšîm — 'when Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of the three men' — rāʾāh (to see, with understanding); ʾên maʿăneh bĕpî (there is no answer in the mouth); the three had not merely paused but had genuinely exhausted their argumentative resources. wayyiḥar ʾappô — 'his anger was aroused' — the second use of wayyiḥar ʾappô (vv2-5 use it three times for the three objects of his anger); the compressed triple use creates a sense of pent-up heat about to release.
6: ʾănî ṣāʿîr lĕyāmîm wĕʾatttem yĕšîšîm — 'I am young in days, and you are very old' — ṣāʿîr (young/junior); yĕšîšîm (the most advanced elders, from yāšēš = to grow very old; used only here and 15:10; a super-aged plural). ʿal-kēn zāḥaltî wāʾîrāʾ mēḥawwōṯ daʿtî ʾetḵem — 'therefore I was afraid and held back from telling you my opinion' — zāḥal (to shrink back/withdraw; used in Deut 32:24 for a serpent-like shrinking; rare); ḥawwāh + daʿat = declaring/showing knowledge. Elihu explicitly narrates his process: age-deference overcoming the urgency of what he had to say.
7: ʾāmartî yāmîm yĕdabbĕrû — 'I thought, let the aged speak' — the conventional wisdom protocol that age brings wisdom (cf. 12:12's same maxim, which Job both cites and subverts); Elihu has been operating by this protocol and finding it inadequate. wĕrōḇ šānîm yôḏîʿû ḥāḵmāh — 'and many years teach wisdom' — rōḇ šānîm (multitude of years); yôḏîʿû (make known/teach); the conventional claim Elihu is about to challenge in v8.
8: ʾāḵēn rûaḥ-hîʾ ḇĕʾĕnôš — 'but truly, it is the Spirit in a person' — ʾāḵēn (truly/surely, a strong adversative contra the preceding maxim); rûaḥ (spirit/breath, the animating principle); bĕʾĕnôš (in man/humankind). wĕnišmaṯ Šaddai tĕḇînēm — 'and the breath of Shaddai gives understanding' — nišmāh (breath; used in Gen 2:7 for God's breath that animated Adam: 'the LORD God breathed into his nostrils the nišmaṯ ḥayyîm'); Šaddai (El Shaddai, the most common divine name in Job's poetry); tĕḇînēm (makes them understand; from bîn). The verse grounds human understanding not in accumulated experience (age) but in the divine-breath given at creation — a direct link between anthropology (creation) and epistemology. John 20:22's inbreathing of the Spirit ('receive the Holy Spirit') re-enacts this creational breath for the new creation community.
9: lō-rabbîm yaḥkāmû — 'it is not the many/great ones who are wise' — rabbîm (many, or 'the great ones'); ḥāḵam (to be wise); the wisdom-egalitarianism of v8 has a direct corollary: greatness and seniority do not automatically confer wisdom. ûzĕqēnîm yāḇînû mišpāṭ — 'nor do the aged always understand what is right' — zĕqēnîm (elders/aged); yāḇîn (understand; the key Elihu verb from bîn); mišpāṭ (justice/right judgment). The verse decouples age and wisdom, seniority and judgment — a remarkable counter-cultural statement in an honor-culture that venerates elders.
10: lāḵēn ʾāmartî šimĕʿû-lî — 'therefore I say: listen to me' — lāḵēn (therefore) draws the logical conclusion from vv8-9: since wisdom comes from divine breath rather than age, Elihu has as legitimate a claim to be heard as the elders. ʾăḥawweh daʿtî ap̄-ʾānî — 'I too will state what I know' — ḥawwāh (to declare/show; same root as v6 and v17); ap̄-ʾānî ('even I' — the emphatic first person singular that refuses to accept automatic disqualification by youth). The claim is grounded in the creation-breath of v8, not in presumption.
11: hēn ḥikkîṯî lĕdiḇrêḵem — 'look, I waited for your words' — ḥikkîṯî (I waited; from ḥāḵāh, the verb of eager waiting used in v4); the formal acknowledgment that Elihu has fulfilled the protocol of waiting. ʾăḇîn ʿaḏ-taḵlîyûn millîm — 'I listened until you had finished your arguments' — ʾăḇîn (I was understanding/paying attention; from bîn in the imperfect used as a past narrative); taḵlîyûn (you completed; from kālāh = to finish/complete); the auditory attentiveness Elihu claims to have maintained throughout the dialogue.
12: wĕʿāḏêhem ʾeṯbônān — 'and I listened carefully to all of you' — hitbônen (to direct one's understanding toward, to give careful attention; the hitpa'el of bîn expressing reflexive and intensive understanding — the same verb as 'staring at' in 30:20). wĕhinnēh ʾên lĕʾîyôḇ mōḵîaḥ — 'and not one of you has refuted Job' — mōḵîaḥ (one who argues/refutes; the hiph'il participle of yāḵaḥ — the forensic-argument root used throughout Job; 13:3,15; 16:21; 23:7); the charge against the three friends: they spoke at length but never actually engaged Job's argument at the forensic level.
13: pen-tōʾmĕrû māṣāʾnû ḥāḵmāh — 'do not say: we have found wisdom' — ʾāmar + māṣāʾ ḥāḵmāh (to claim discovery of wisdom); Elihu warns the friends against the self-consoling claim that their failure to answer Job is actually a sign of superior wisdom. ʾēl yiddĕp̄ennû lōʾ ʾîš — 'let God deal with him, not us' — yiddĕp̄eh (from nādap̄ = to drive away/thrust aside; rare); the dismissive passing of Job's case back to God as an evasion of the friends' responsibility. Elihu refuses to allow this theological exit strategy.
14: wĕlō-ʿāraḵ ʾēlay millîm — 'he has not directed his argument against me' — ʿārak (to arrange/order, the legal-brief word; cf. 13:18 'I have ordered/ʿāraktî my case'); Job has not targeted his argument at Elihu specifically. wĕḇdibĕrêḵem lōʾ ʾăšîḇennû — 'and I will not reply to him with your kind of arguments' — the formal distancing from the three friends' approach: Elihu commits in advance to a different argumentative strategy. The verse establishes Elihu as a genuinely independent voice, not merely a fourth iteration of the retributive framework.
15: ḥattû lōʾ-ʿānû ʿôd — 'they are astonished, they say nothing more' — ḥāṯat (to be dismayed/shattered; the verb of complete psychological collapse under overwhelming evidence or divine presence; Josh 1:9; Isa 31:4); the three friends have been silenced not by persuasion but by the weight of Job's unanswered arguments. nāśĕʿû mēhem millîm — 'words have left them' — nāśāʾ (to carry away/lift up); millîm (words/arguments); the arguments have been carried away, leaving the friends empty-handed.
16: wĕhôḥaltî kî-lō yĕdabbĕrû — 'I waited because they did not speak' — ḥûl/ḥîl (to wait/writhe in labor-pains; the word for the birthing-labor used in Ps 22:9-10 and Isa 26:17-18); Elihu's waiting is not passive but like labor contractions. kî ʿāmĕḏû lō-ʿānû ʿôd — 'they stood there and answered no more' — ʿāmaḏ (to stand/remain standing); the three friends standing speechless is a visual tableau of intellectual defeat.
17: ʾaʿăneh ʾap̄-ʾānî ḥelqî — 'then I said: I too will give my answer, my portion' — ḥēleq (portion/allotment; the same word as the divine 'portion from above' in 31:2); Elihu's turn to speak is his appointed ḥēleq — his divinely allotted portion of the dialogue. ʾăḥawweh daʿtî ʾānî — 'I too will state what I know' — a slight variation of v10b; the repetition creates a rhetorical frame around the waiting-and-watching section (vv10-17) that preceded his actual speech.
18: kî māleʾtî millîm — 'for I am full of words' — māleʾ (to be full/filled; the fullness-of-speech word); millîm (words). hĕṣîqatnî rûaḥ ḇiṭnî — 'the spirit within me urges me on' — hēṣîq (to press/constrain; from ṣûq = to press/squeeze; used in Jer 19:9 for siege-pressure); rûaḥ (spirit, picking up the rûaḥ of v8's creation-breath; Elihu's inner compulsion to speak is the action of the same Spirit that gives understanding); ḇeṭen (belly/interior, the deep inner self). The compulsion to speak from an inner spiritual pressure is exactly what the NT prophets describe (1 Cor 9:16 'necessity is laid upon me'; Acts 4:20 'we cannot but speak').
19: hinnēh-ḇiṭnî kĕyayin lō-yippāṯēaḥ — 'my belly is like wine that has no outlet' — ḇeṭen (the inner vessel); yayyin (wine); pāṯaḥ (to open); the fermentation-pressure metaphor: wine sealed without a vent will burst the container. kĕʾōḇôṯ ḥăḏāšîm yibbāqēaʿ — 'like new wineskins about to burst' — ʾōḇ (wineskin/bellows; a skin bag); ḥăḏāšîm (new/fresh); bāqaʿ (to split/burst open). The new-wineskin image is used by Jesus in Matt 9:17 / Mark 2:22 / Luke 5:37-38 for the new wine of the kingdom that cannot be contained in old wineskins — the gospel's explosive new content demanding new forms. Elihu's metaphor prefigures the kind of pressure that the divine speech from the whirlwind will exert.
20: ʾăḏabbĕrāh wĕyirwaḥ lî — 'I will speak and find relief' — rāwaḥ (to breathe/find relief; from rûaḥ = breath/spirit; the physical relief of releasing pent-up breath as metaphor for finally speaking what has been held back). ʾep̄tāḥ śĕp̄āṯay wĕʾeʿĕneh — 'I will open my lips and answer' — the opening of lips as the formal beginning of speech; pātaḥ (to open); the verse signals Elihu's transition from preamble to the actual content of his argument that begins in ch33.
21: ʾal-nāʾ ʾeśśāʾ p̄ĕnê-ʾîš — 'I will not show partiality to any person' — nāśāʾ p̄ānîm (to lift the face/show favoritism; the judicial corruption idiom; Lev 19:15 prohibits it explicitly); Job has accused the friends of divine advocacy through partiality (13:8); Elihu renounces it in advance. wĕʾel-ʾādām lō ʾăḵanneh — 'nor will I give flattering titles to any man' — kinnāh (to give a title/honorific to; from kinnûy = nickname/epithet; rare). Elihu's commitment to titles-free, partiality-free speech sets up the authority of what follows — his argument stands on its merits, not on rhetorical deference.
22: kî lō yāḏaʿtî ʾăḵanneh — 'for I do not know how to flatter' — the confession of incapacity for flattery as a mark of integrity; yāḏaʿ (to know how); ʾăḵanneh (to give honorific titles, from v21). kimʿaṭ yiśśāʾēnî ʿōśenî — 'if I did, my Maker would soon take me away' — kimʿaṭ (quickly/soon; the word of almost-immediacy); nāśāʾ (to carry away/remove); ʿōśenî (my Maker; from ʿāśāh = to make/do). The ground of Elihu's integrity is theological accountability: to flatter would be to court immediate divine removal. The chapter ends with the same divine Maker who gave the understanding-breath (v8) serving as the guarantor of honest speech.
